policy_statement: Convene a work group to adopt climate resilient design guidelines for State-funded projects (OGS and DEC).
mechanism_description: OGS and DEC convene a work group to adopt climate resilient design guidelines for state-funded projects, helping reduce infrastructure vulnerability to climate hazards.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → resilient design standards applied to infrastructure → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ damage/service disruption
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human/system change: Public agencies adopt and apply climate-resilient design guidelines (e.g., updated design loads, siting criteria, freeboard, heat tolerances) to state-funded capital projects and asset upgrades. (2) Mediating process: Infrastructure is engineered/located/constructed to tolerate projected climate stressors, reducing failure probability under extreme events and chronic changes. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower climate risk—fewer asset losses, reduced downtime of critical services, and reduced recovery needs during/after floods, storms, heat waves, and sea-level rise impacts.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced damage and service disruption from climate hazards due to more resilient infrastructure design standards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards acting on built assets are the starting point, and the policy intervenes by improving design/standards to reduce vulnerability and expected damages. 2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: the primary intent and effect is risk reduction and continuity of services, not emissions reduction (Mitigation), reduced resource inputs (Resource Efficiency), or ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions). 3) Edge cases applied: resilient design can sometimes include energy-efficiency or low-carbon materials (mitigation co-benefits), but those are not inherent to “climate resilient design guidelines” and are secondary unless explicitly specified. 4) Applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: any policy adopting climate-resilient design guidelines for infrastructure is fundamentally an adaptation measure aimed at reducing hazard impacts on assets and services. 5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence (e.g., whether guidelines target flooding vs heat, critical infrastructure vs buildings, or include explicit mitigation requirements).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “resilient design guidelines” explicitly mandate low-carbon construction, electrification, or energy efficiency as the core requirement, mitigation could become dominant; otherwise, the mechanism is straightforwardly adaptation-focused.
key_indicators: "convene a work group"; "adopt climate resilient design guidelines"; "State-funded projects"; "OGS and DEC"
co_benefits: Improved asset lifecycle performance and reliability; potential cost savings from avoided damage and downtime.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be coded as a performance-standard or regulatory-mandate if the guidelines are explicitly required/mandatory for all state-funded projects; the text emphasizes convening/adoption process, so treated as enabling planning/strategy.
